text: Is there any difference between "real OO" as described by Kay, and the "actor model"?
Is there any difference between "real OO" as described by Kay, and the "actor model"?

Seems like the same thing with a different name. But suppossedly the "actor" model lets youd eal with concurrency naturally. I don't buy it, since the real consistency problems arise on a higher abstraction level than the explicit code implementation.

Seems like the same thing with a different name. But suppossedly the "actor" model lets youd eal with concurrency naturally. I don't buy it, since the real consistency problems arise on a higher abstraction level than the explicit code implementation.

I use the actor model in erlang and I really get concurrency for free. The only problem with "objects are just actors" is that if you do the blocking receive on a mailbox then you can't "call yourself" or a call chain can't call one of the callers since that's a deadlock.

I'd like to announce a new channel: <#CJT25RWKE|research-center>.

Until I moved away from SF last year, I was an organizing part of a monthly meetup to discuss the future of coding.  At the time, I thought about creating a long weekend or week long get-away for the group + others (say 30 people). But in the end, I was afraid a week wouldn't be enough time to do enough development to flesh out ideas. And then it would wind up being a seven-day version of our the discussion meetings, where we would debate based on what we already have found, rather than _what we are currently finding_.

That led me to another idea: _what if it was a year_?  What if the location was far away from everything else -- a sort of Manhattan Project for the Future of Coding?  30 people, more or less, working on maybe 20-40 ideas.  Researchers would have full ownership of their work. Milestones not deadlines. No specific targets. Vision not goals.  Weekly reviews of progress and critical peer sessions (ala PARC).  Baseball, not golf (to quote Alan Kay).

Is the Comprehensible FRP talk online somewhere?
Is the Comprehensible FRP talk online somewhere?

Huh... is the difference here that something like Smalltalk uses some isolation in the form of the call stack, which makes some things easier (like recursion and some state kept on the stack) but also doesn't allow implicit concurrency or create the firmer boundaries of Actors? (I'm not sure, just trying to puzzle out the difference)

Huh... is the difference here that something like Smalltalk uses some isolation in the form of the call stack, which makes some things easier (like recursion and some state kept on the stack) but also doesn't allow implicit concurrency or create the firmer boundaries of Actors? (I'm not sure, just trying to puzzle out the difference)

yes, an executable spec is just a very concise programming language that leans towards the declarative, and has a minimum of "fat" in terms of extraneous words. I measure projects by how many words are typed into the computer, as line count is highly misleading due to some languages being more vertical than horizontal. I consider spec-only languages to be a giant waste of time because with almost the same level of effort you can build the product, so why bother with a non-executable intermediary? The other objection i have with TLA+ is that it doesn't include a graphical model, and since the majority of words of code in a typical graphical interactive product relates to drawing, specifications which don't cover visual appearance are thus only partial specifications, and not conducive to improving productivity.

Hello all. I figure it’s time I should formally introduce myself. I’m a programmer in Virginia who’s eagerly looking for the future of coding. My dream is to unlock the power of computing for non-programmers. I’ve seen this done particularly well in Dynamics CRM, Microsoft Excel, and in the now-abandoned Eve language. I’m particularly interested in logic programming, query languages (like Datalog), and the semantic web, so naturally I pay close attention to Dustin Getz’ Hyperfiddle project. Clojure is my weapon of choice, but I’m developing an appreciation for static types and the expressivity they bring, and am interested in exploring formal methods and dependently-typed languages like Idris. I also have an avid interest in how philosophy bears on computation, and how a humane philosophy can work itself out into humane software. I’m very grateful to have found this group. Here’s to the future of coding :clinking_glasses:

I’m undertaking creating a gamified chore list for my family. With zero experience in games, what’s a good way to get started with animating sprites and such? Ideally I want to write the “business rules” and user interface in my language of choice (ClojureScript), but I want something I can easily express art to draw and animate little sprites (like Adobe Photoshop). Suggestions?

(Not that I'm saying I'm solving any of it yet, but that's definitely the direction I'm heading)
Looking forward to sharing first results with you in a few weeks :grin:

text: Oh, and if yo're wondering: My Haskell-ish programming language for it is PureScript. Compiles to JavaScript and can therefore be run in the Browser and on the Server. Also a C target is in the works…
